prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.
 
 Please base your answer only on the information in this message.
 
 About me: I'm a 29-year-old man.
 
 My situation:
 - Symptoms: I had routine bloodwork and feel fine. I had a bad cold about 2 weeks ago that has resolved. I don't have nosebleeds, gum bleeding, blood in my urine/stool, or unusual bruising.
 - History: No blood thinners or new medications.
 - Vitals: BP 112/70, HR 74
 - Exam: No abnormal exam findings reported.
 
 My labs (with units and reference ranges):
 - Platelets: 128 x10^3/uL (150-400) LOW
 - Hemoglobin: 13.1 g/dL (12.0-16.0) normal
 - WBC: 6.8 x10^3/uL (4.5-11.0) normal
 - INR: 1.0 (0.9-1.1) normal
 
 These results just posted to my patient portal.
 
 Please answer in exactly this format:
 
 EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>
 
 TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D - choose ONE letter only>
 - A: Fine to monitor at home
 - B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
 - C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
 - D: Go to the ER now
 
 CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>
 
 If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your platelet count is mildly low at 128, just below the normal lower limit of 150. Everything else — hemoglobin, white blood cells, and clotting (INR) — looks perfectly normal. Importantly, you have no bleeding symptoms at all (no bruising, nosebleeds, blood in stool/urine, or gum bleeding), and you feel well.

A very common cause of a mildly low platelet count in a young, healthy person is a recent viral infection — and you had a bad cold about 2 weeks ago. Viruses can temporarily suppress platelet production or increase platelet turnover. In most cases, the count bounces back to normal within a few weeks after the infection resolves.

This is not an emergency, but it's worth having your doctor review the result and likely recheck your platelets in a few weeks to confirm they're recovering.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 92%

Viral illnesses can temporarily suppress platelet production or increase platelet destruction, and counts typically recover on their own within a few weeks. This is worth having your doctor review and recheck in a few weeks to confirm recovery, but it is not an emergency.

When your body fights off a virus, platelets can temporarily decrease and typically recover on their own over a few weeks. Since you feel well, have no bleeding symptoms, and the count is only slightly below normal (not in a dangerous range), this is unlikely to represent anything serious.
